perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information. VENDOR acknowledges that in the course of providing services to BPS and its students , only VENDOR and its employees or representatives will be given or have access to certain directory, routing, and confidential student information (“Confidential Information”) which may typically protected from disclosure with or without prior consent by va rious laws including the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) 20 U.S.C. § 1232g et seq., 34 C.F.R. § 99 et seq., and 603 C.M.R. 23.00 et seq. VENDOR acknowledges and agrees to comply fully with these federal and state privacy and student recor d confidentiality laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information. VENDOR acknowledges that in the course of providing services to BPS and its students , only VENDOR and its employees or representatives will be given or have access to certain directory, routing, and confidential student information (“Confidential Information”) which is not typically protected from disclosure with or without prior consent by va rious laws including the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) 20 U.S.C. § 1232g et seq., 34 C.F.R. § 99 et seq., and 603 C.M.R. 23.00 et seq. VENDOR acknowledges and agrees to comply fully with these federal and state privacy and student recor d confidentiality laws.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that Confidential Information is 'not typically protected' the contract directly contradicts the purpose and requirements of FERPA, which mandates the protection of student records.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: FERPA requires educational agencies and institutions to protect the privacy of student education records. By stating that the information is not typically protected, the contract creates a direct conflict with the legal requirements of FERPA.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that confidential student information is 'not typically protected,' directly contradicting the purpose of FERPA, which requires the protection of student education records. The original text acknowledged that this information 'may typically protected from disclosure' - but this was changed. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 20 U.S.C. 1232g, a key component of FERPA, indicating that the act provides specific legal protections for student records, which the changed text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Reasonable security precautions and protections include, but are not limited to: a) Creating, distributing, and implementing data governance policies and procedure s which protect Confidential Information through appropriate administrative, technical, and physical security safeguards, and outline staff responsibilities for maintaining data security.
perturbation.changed_text: Reasonable security precautions and protections include, but are not limited to: a) Creating, distributing, and implementing data governance policies and procedure s which may protect Confidential Information through suggested administrative, technical, and physical security safeguards, and loosely outline staff responsibilities for maintaining data security.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'protect' to 'may protect', 'appropriate' to 'suggested', and 'outline' to 'loosely outline' weakens the security requirements and creates uncertainty about whether the security measures are mandatory or optional.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93H requires businesses to implement and maintain a comprehensive information security program. By suggesting that security measures are optional or loosely defined, the modified contract contradicts the mandatory security requirements of this law.
perturbation.location: Section B: Terms of Agreement, Paragraph 1

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth | Mass.gov An official website of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts Here's how you know Official websites use .mass.gov A .mass.gov website belongs to an official government organization in Massachusetts. Secure websites use HTTPS certificate A lock icon ( ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the official website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. This page, 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth, is offered by Trial Court Law Libraries show more Massachusetts Court System This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR + This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR × Table of Contents 201 CMR 10.00: Weighing, sorting and culling of fish 201 CMR 11.00: New and used motor vehicle arbitration 201 CMR 12.00: Massachusetts do not call registry 201 CMR 14.00: Home Improvement Contractor Arbitration and Guaranty Fund 201 CMR 16.00: Placing, lifting and removal of security freezes 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth 201 CMR 18.00: Registration and enforcement of Home Improvement Contractor program 201 CMR 19.00: Customized wheelchair arbitration Regulation Regulation 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth Date: 11/13/2009 Organization: Office of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation Regulatory Authority: MGL c. 93H Official Version: Purchase the official print version 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information about a resident of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in connection with the safeguarding of personal information contained in both paper and electronic records, to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information in a manner fully consistent with industry standards; protect against anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of such information; and protect against unauthorized access to or use of such information that may result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any consumer. perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the security requirements. The original text requires 'appropriate administrative, technical, and physical security safeguards' to 'protect Confidential Information', while the changed text only 'may protect Confidential Information through suggested administrative, technical, and physical security safeguards'. This introduces uncertainty, as the security measures are no longer mandatory. The law explanation states that Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93H requires businesses to implement and maintain a comprehensive information security program, and the modified contract contradicts the mandatory security requirements of this law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 201 CMR 17.00, establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information.

perturbation.original_text: VENDOR shall maintain the privacy of protected personal information and shall be financially responsible, if and to the extent that any security breach relating to protected personal information results from acts or omissions of VENDOR , its employees and/or repres entatives for any notifications to affected persons (after prompt consultation with BPS), and to the extent requested by BPS, administratively responsible for such notification.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR may maintain the privacy of protected personal information and may be financially responsible, if and to the extent that any security breach relating to protected personal information results from gross negligence of VENDOR , its employees and/or repres entatives for any notifications to affected persons (after prompt consultation with BPS), and to the extent requested by BPS, administratively responsible for such notification.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' and adding 'gross negligence' introduces ambiguity about the vendor's responsibility for data breaches, potentially conflicting with data breach notification laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Data Breach Law requires notification of security breaches. By changing 'shall' to 'may' and requiring 'gross negligence,' the modified contract weakens the mandatory notification requirements and raises the threshold for vendor liability, conflicting with the law's intent.
perturbation.location: Section B: Terms of Agreement, Paragraph 2

perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'shall,' indicating a mandatory obligation for the vendor to maintain privacy and be financially responsible in case of a data breach. The changed text replaces 'shall' with 'may,' making these obligations discretionary. Furthermore, it introduces the requirement of 'gross negligence' to trigger financial responsibility. This directly contradicts the Massachusetts Data Breach Law, as explained in the Law Explanation, which requires notification of security breaches. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from Chapter 93H, concerns 'SECURITY BREACHES' and Section 3 covers the 'Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information.' The modification weakens the vendor's mandatory notification requirements and increases the threshold for liability, creating a clear contradiction with the intent of the law to protect personal information, since the new text now allows for breaches that do not involve gross negligence to potentially go unreported or unaddressed financially. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 201 CMR 17.00, establishes 'minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information' which suggests that the 'may' statement does not align, resulting in a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that all acti vity pursuant to this Agreement shall be in accordance with this Agreement and all applicable current or future federal, state, a nd local laws, and all applicable rules and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that all acti vity pursuant to this Agreement should endeavor to be in accordance with this Agreement and all applicable current or future federal, state, a nd local laws, and all applicable rules and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be' to 'should endeavor to be' weakens the commitment to legal compliance, suggesting that adherence to laws is optional rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: General contract law requires that agreements be formed with the intent to create legal obligations. By stating the parties should 'endeavor to be' in compliance with laws, this weakens the promise to abide by applicable laws and could void the agreement.
perturbation.location: Section B: Terms of Agreement, Paragraph 16
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The original contract language states the parties 'shall be' in accordance with applicable laws, indicating a mandatory obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'should endeavor to be', which suggests compliance is optional. This contradicts the principle of contract law that agreements should create mutual obligations enforceable by law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2: 'A contract is an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law'. By weakening the commitment to legal compliance, the changed text undermines a fundamental aspect of contract law, making the agreement potentially voidable. The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the enforceability of legal compliance, a core element of a binding contract.
